behaviors.description: Out-of-stock products excluded from recommendation results
behaviors.justification: Filtering out-of-stock items requires integrating with the product catalog schema and database queries. A Unit test mocking the filter function would miss real failures in data access (availability field not being read), schema mismatches, or query construction (WHERE clause missing). The failure surface is at the data integration boundary between filtering logic and catalog storage.
behaviors.test_description: Create products in test database with mixed availability states, call recommendation API, verify out-of-stock products do not appear in returned results while in-stock products do.

behaviors.description: User preference filter excludes blocked product categories
behaviors.justification: Excluding blocked categories requires reading user preference settings from persistent storage and cross-referencing them with product categories in the catalog. Unit tests with mocked preferences miss real failures in preference retrieval, schema mapping, or category matching across the integration boundary. The actual failure occurs when settings are not read correctly or categories are not matched properly.
behaviors.test_description: Create user with blocked categories in settings, add products to various categories in catalog, call recommendation API with user context, verify no products from blocked categories appear in results.

behaviors.description: Algorithm ranks products matching recent browsing higher than no-signal products
behaviors.justification: The ranking algorithm depends on correctly retrieving user browsing history from storage and weighting it appropriately against other signals. While the ranking formula itself could be unit-tested, real failures occur in how browsing events are queried, structured, and integrated into the pipeline. A Unit test with perfectly formatted mock history would miss failures in history retrieval, schema mapping, or weight application across systems.
behaviors.test_description: Create user with browsing history in specific product categories, add products to browsed and unbrowsed categories, call recommendation API, verify products from browsed categories rank higher in results than products with no browsing signal.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns correct page with stable ordering across requests
behaviors.justification: Stable pagination requires consistent database query ordering across multiple executions with unchanged data. Unit tests with in-memory mocked result sets cannot catch database-level non-determinism, unstable sort orders, cursor state issues, or index selection changes that only emerge with real data and actual database queries. The failure is at the database query stability boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Call recommendation API requesting page 2 twice with no intervening user activity or data changes, verify identical result sets and ordering are returned both times.

behaviors.description: Algorithm surfaces at least one product from unbrowsed category (serendipity requirement)
behaviors.justification: Serendipity requires correctly identifying user browsing history, mapping it to product categories, and selecting from the unbrowsed complement set. Real failures occur when browsing history is not retrieved correctly, category mapping is wrong, or the selection logic fails to trigger. Unit tests with mocked history miss failures in history retrieval, category schema mapping, and integration of the serendipity check into the ranking pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Create user with browsing history in 2-3 product categories, add products spanning 4+ different categories, call recommendation API, verify results include at least one product from a category user has not browsed before.

behaviors.description: New user with no history receives popularity-based recommendations
behaviors.justification: The cold-start fallback requires correct identification of new-user state (no purchase/browsing history) and access to accurate popularity metrics. Unit tests with mocked popularity scores miss real failures in state detection logic, popularity metric calculation or staleness, and fallback trigger conditions. The failure surface is at the integration of state detection and metric retrieval.
behaviors.test_description: Call recommendation API for new user with no purchase or browsing history, verify result set is non-empty and products are ranked by overall popularity metrics.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records correct product ID, position, and timestamp
behaviors.justification: Recording clicks requires persisting data to a real database or event log with correct schema and indexing. Unit tests mocking the persistence layer verify only that the correct data is passed to the persistence function, not that the data is actually written, stored, indexed, or retrievable. Real failures occur in the persistence layer: schema mismatches, failed writes, incorrect field mapping, or timestamp handling. The failure is at the persistence boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Call recommendation API to get results, simulate user click on a specific product in the response, query click log/database, verify the click was recorded with correct product_id, position in result list, and accurate timestamp.

behaviors.description: Response time stays under 200ms for catalog of 100,000 products
behaviors.justification: Performance requirements cannot be verified at Unit level—in-process mocked data executes in microseconds and hides algorithmic complexity, inefficient queries, and scaling failures. The real failure only emerges at realistic data volume. Integration tests with 100k products in a test database expose O(n²) algorithms, missing indexes, or inefficient joins that exceed 200ms. System-level testing with real network/infrastructure adds overhead but Integration with realistic data is the minimum to catch the algorithmic failure.
behaviors.test_description: Load 100,000 products into test database with realistic schema, call recommendation API for typical user, measure end-to-end response time, verify it completes in under 200ms.

behaviors.description: Purchased product deprioritized in subsequent recommendation requests
behaviors.justification: Deprioritization requires the algorithm to read purchase history from persistent storage, recognize purchase state changes, and apply weighting adjustments. Unit tests with mocked purchase history miss real failures in purchase tracking, state persistence, history retrieval, or weight application across the integration boundary. The failure occurs when purchase events are not persisted correctly, not retrieved in time, or not integrated into the ranking calculation.
behaviors.test_description: Create user with purchase history, call recommendation API, verify previously purchased products appear lower in ranking than unpurchased products with similar relevance scores.

behaviors.description: Results are deterministic for same user state (identical results on repeated requests)
behaviors.justification: Determinism with unchanged user state requires consistent database query ordering and no non-deterministic elements in the algorithm. Unit tests with seeded randomness or in-memory data cannot catch database-level ordering inconsistencies, cursor behavior changes, or runtime non-determinism that emerges with real data and actual queries. The failure surface is at the data consistency boundary—whether the same query returns the same order across invocations.
behaviors.test_description: Call recommendation API twice for same user state with no intervening user activity or data changes, verify identical ranked product list is returned both times.
